categories.title: YOUNG ONES

categories.title: IRON IN SCIENCE

clues.clue: Magnetite, noted for its natural magnetism, is an important ore of this metal.

categories.title: WHAT DOES IT STAND FOR?

clues.clue: In networking, this four-letter acronym stands for 'Open Shortest Path First', a routing protocol.
clues.fit_justification: Source question asks 'In networking, what does OSPF stand for?' and source answer is 'Open Shortest Path First'. Both acronym and expansion appear literally in source text.

categories.title: BODY OF KNOWLEDGE

finals.title: COSMIC DISTANCES
finals.clue: This substance's very existence is inferred only from its gravitational effects on visible matter — it has never been directly detected, yet it is thought to make up most of the universe's mass.

finals.title: NAMING THE MOLECULE
finals.clue: Though its chemical name is isobutylphenylpropanoic acid, you likely have this common over-the-counter painkiller in your medicine cabinet right now.

finals.title: SCIENCE FINAL
finals.clue: In 1905, German scientist Alfred Einhorn synthesized this first injectable local anesthetic used in dentistry, a name still recognized today even as it has been largely replaced by newer drugs.
finals.fit_justification: Source question states 'In 1905, German scientist Alfred Einhorn created the first injectable local anesthetic used in dentistry. What is it called?' and source answer is 'Novocaine'. All facts in the clue are drawn directly from source text.

finals.title: ASTRONOMY
finals.clue: Two separate source facts share this as the answer: one identifies it as the cause of the annual Orionids meteor shower each October, and the other names the subject of the 1986 Giotto space probe mission.
finals.fit_justification: Source question asks 'Which comet causes the Orionids meteor shower each October?' and source answer is 'Halley's Comet'; a second fact (tapi:645c9f1642ea81e2554c5eeb) also has answer 'Halley's Comet' for the 1986 Giotto probe, confirming the answer's notability without duplicating a category clue.

finals.title: SCIENCE FINAL
finals.clue: This physicist's landmark 1995 proof resolved a 358-year-old number theory puzzle first posed by a 17th-century French mathematician, earning him multiple major awards.
finals.fit_justification: Source question asks 'Who proved Fermat's Last Theorem?' and source answer is 'Andrew Wiles'; the fact is hard enough for a final clue and requires specific knowledge of mathematical history.

finals.title: FAMOUS SCIENTISTS
finals.clue: This Scottish bacteriologist, working at St. Mary's Hospital in London in 1928, famously noticed that a mold was killing bacteria in a contaminated petri dish, leading to a world-changing medical discovery.
finals.fit_justification: Source question asks 'Who Is Accepted As The Discoverer Of Penicillin?' and source answer is 'Alexander Fleming'.

finals.title: SCIENTIFIC FIRSTS
finals.clue: This German chemist won the 1944 Nobel Prize for Chemistry for discovering nuclear fission, though the honor was controversial given the wartime context of his work.
finals.fit_justification: Source question: 'Which German Chemist Won The Nobel Prize For Chemistry In 1944 For Discovering Nuclear Fission?' — directly identifies a specific scientist and achievement; answer is 'Otto Hahn'.

finals.title: NUCLEAR DISCOVERY
finals.clue: In a landmark 1938 experiment, Otto Hahn, Lise Meitner, and Fritz Strassmann bombarded atoms of this heavy element to achieve the first confirmed instance of nuclear fission.
finals.fit_justification: Source question explicitly names 'Otto Hahn, Lise Meitner, and Fritz Strassman' and asks which element they bombarded 'to discover nuclear fission'; source answer is 'Uranium'.
